perturbation.original_text: Thereafter, the Maintenance Term shall automatically renew for successive period, 12 months, on the terms and conditions of this Agreement unless Bank of America terminates Maintenance Services pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Thereafter, the Maintenance Term may renew for a successive period, potentially 12 months, subject to vaguely defined terms and conditions, unless Bank of America expresses an intention to terminate Maintenance Services.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall automatically renew' with 'may renew' and introduces uncertainty about the term length ('potentially 12 months'). The 'vaguely defined terms and conditions' introduces ambiguity, making the renewal process unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification when an agreement's duration is indefinite. By making the renewal terms vague, the modified text potentially allows for termination without reasonable notification, contradicting the UCC's requirement for good faith and commercial reasonableness in contract termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the renewal terms and duration of the Maintenance Term, potentially contradicting UCC § 2-309. The original text states the term 'shall automatically renew', while the changed text uses 'may renew' and 'potentially 12 months', creating uncertainty. According to UCC § 2-309(3), termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification', and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if unconscionable. By introducing vague terms and conditions, the changed text could allow for termination without reasonable notification, undermining the UCC's requirement.

perturbation.original_text: During the initial Maintenance Term and any renewal term, Maintenance Fees shall be paid in the increments described below under "Payment Terms."
perturbation.changed_text: During the initial Maintenance Term and any renewal term, Maintenance Fees will ideally be paid according to the approximate increments alluded to under "Payment Terms."
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be paid' to 'will ideally be paid' weakens the payment obligation. Replacing 'described' with 'approximate increments alluded to' introduces ambiguity regarding the fee structure and payment schedule, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305 addresses open price terms in contracts, requiring a good faith standard. By making the payment increments 'approximate' and 'alluded to', the modification creates an open price term with undefined parameters, potentially violating the good faith requirement for determining price, and is therefore difficult to enforce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the payment of maintenance fees in specified increments ('shall be paid'). The changed text weakens this obligation by stating fees 'will ideally be paid' according to 'approximate increments alluded to'. This introduces ambiguity about the exact payment terms, potentially leading to disputes and enforcement difficulties. UCC § 2-305(1) allows contracts even when the price isn't settled, but it stipulates a 'reasonable price' must be determined. By using terms like 'approximate' and 'alluded to', the changed text fails to provide a clear basis for determining a reasonable price, therefore contradicting the good faith requirement inherent in establishing payment terms under the UCC, especially in absence of any other price fixing mechanism.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall provide the following training classes pursuant to this Agreement in connection with installation of the first copy of the Software.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier may, if feasible, provide some training classes, perhaps related to the Agreement, and possibly in connection with the installation of the first copy of the Software.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the obligation to provide training. Replacing 'shall' with 'may' makes the training discretionary. The addition of phrases like 'if feasible', 'some training classes', 'perhaps related to', and 'possibly in connection with' introduces multiple layers of ambiguity, making the obligation unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-311. Options and Cooperation Respecting Performance.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-311 addresses options and cooperation regarding performance. By making the provision of training highly discretionary and vague, the modified text undermines the requirement for cooperation in performance by giving the supplier excessive leeway and creating uncertainty for the receiving party, rendering it unenforceable without further clarification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-311. Options and Cooperation Respecting Performance. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-311. Options and Cooperation Respecting Performance. (1) An agreement for sale which is otherwise sufficiently definite (subsection (3) of Section 2-204 ) to be a contract is not made invalid by the fact that it leaves particulars of performance to be specified by one of the parties. Any such specification must be made in good faith and within limits set by commercial reasonableness. (2) Unless otherwise agreed specifications relating to assortment of the goods are at the buyer's option and except as otherwise provided in subsections (1)(c) and (3) of Section 2-319 specifications or arrangements relating to shipment are at the seller's option. (3)Where such specification would materially affect the other party's performance but is not seasonably made or where one party's cooperation is necessary to the agreed performance of the other but is not seasonably forthcoming, the other party in addition to all other remedies (a) is excused for any resulting delay in his own performance; and (b) may also either proceed to perform in any reasonable manner or after the time for a material part of his own performance treat the failure to specify or to cooperate as a breach by failure to deliver or accept the goods . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the supplier to provide training classes ('shall'). The changed text makes this obligation discretionary by using 'may, if feasible' which introduces ambiguity. The Law Explanation states this weakens cooperation in performance under UCC § 2-311. Snippet 1 from UCC § 2-311 outlines cooperation respecting performance, emphasizing that agreements should be sufficiently definite. By making the training discretionary, the modified text undermines the definiteness required for enforceable agreements, creating a legal contradiction as the obligation becomes unenforceable without further clarification.
